caveats: a: No direct six-digit occupation-by-task exposure study was found.
a: The frozen compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and may include business models unlike screened contract or repeat-supply manufacturers.
rho: Implementation evidence for LMM pharmaceutical plants is not representative or publicly standardized.
rho: Sterile, solid-dose, liquid, and specialty preparations have different validation and operational constraints.
e: The assigned 345-firm population is margin-bridged and not an observed EBITDA-band census.
e: FDA establishments, applicants, labels, and operating firms are different units and cannot be counted interchangeably.
s5: No owner-age or succession panel specific to eligible firms was found.
s5: Distressed closures and asset divestitures must be excluded unless a transferable going concern changes control.
q: No representative contract dataset was available.
q: Retention differs between proprietary products, commodity generics, scarce capacity, and sponsor-owned formulations.
d5: CMS forecasts spending rather than constant-quality manufacturing volume.
d5: Retail prescriptions omit institutional and non-retail products and include value unrelated to manufacturing quantity.
o: This measures operator requirement for the current service basket, not whether the same independent operator retains the customer.
o: Some individualized production may migrate to pharmacies or healthcare facilities classified outside this NAICS.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat batch, product, and contract-manufacturing revenue under supply agreements, purchase orders, private-label arrangements, and sponsor quality agreements; pricing is generally per batch, unit, or product rather than per labor hour.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, pure drug-development or IP entities without manufacturing, pharmacies and compounders classified elsewhere, distributors without production, shell applicants, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market manufacturers of finished pharmaceutical preparations serving repeat external drug sponsors, brands, wholesalers, health-system channels, or private-label customers.

a.bridge: BLS occupational data for other chemical-manufacturing groups show a workforce led by equipment, packaging and mixing operators, supervisors, material movers, inspectors, chemists, and managers rather than NAICS 325412 itself. FDA finished-drug guidance documents extensive batch records, calculations, deviations, testing, annual reviews, validation, and control records. I map document search, drafting, review triage, planning, and analysis to AI exposure while excluding physical production, cleaning, testing, maintenance, sampling, and final quality release; the non-pharma occupational proxy may understate professional quality and regulatory work and misstate automation levels.
caveats: No direct six-digit occupation-by-task exposure study was found.
The frozen compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and may include business models unlike screened contract or repeat-supply manufacturers.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census from 20-30 LMM finished-dose manufacturers, separated into production, packaging, laboratory, quality, validation, regulatory, supply-chain, and commercial roles, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Exposed work includes production planning, document retrieval, regulatory and medical-information drafting support, batch-record and deviation triage, supplier-document review, forecasting, procurement analysis, and customer operations. Embodied production and legally accountable quality decisions dominate much of the remaining labor.

d5.bridge: CMS projects retail prescription-drug spending average growth of 4.9% annually for 2026-2033, which is nominal downstream spending rather than constant-price output of LMM preparation manufacturers. I discount the forecast for price and mix, and apply only partial upstream transmission, yielding modest volume growth; it may overstate this basket because high-cost new drugs drive spending and may be made by large or foreign firms, while it may understate non-retail preparations.
caveats: CMS forecasts spending rather than constant-quality manufacturing volume.
Retail prescriptions omit institutional and non-retail products and include value unrelated to manufacturing quantity.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-price shipment and dose-volume forecasts by dosage form and customer type, reconciled to FDA product discontinuations, approvals, shortages, and LMM manufacturer backlogs, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Aging, new therapies, generic utilization, supply resilience, and recurring medicine use support unit demand, but drug-pricing policy, loss of exclusivity, discontinuations, foreign sourcing, formulation changes, and product-specific attrition create wide outcomes. FDA shortage evidence confirms the need for qualified capacity but not aggregate growth.

caveats: The assigned 345-firm population is margin-bridged and not an observed EBITDA-band census.
FDA establishments, applicants, labels, and operating firms are different units and cannot be counted interchangeably.
e.change_evidence: A firm census linking SUSB-band manufacturers to FDA establishment and application records, ownership, customer concentration, external manufacturing revenue, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Independent finished-dose manufacturers generally sell repeatedly to external sponsors, brands, wholesalers, or healthcare channels, so the recurring relationship criterion is common. Captive sites, IP-led developers with incidental manufacturing, non-operating applicants, and firms outside the EBITDA band reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325412 ($33,478,870,324) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325412 ($149,284,530,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $904,272 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325412); class <5: 388 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,016,887 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $363,241 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 143 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,972,531 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $535,353 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 126 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,802,310 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,405,196 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 219 firms, avg revenue/firm $19,526,562 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,516,734 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 173 firms, avg revenue/firm $83,835,855 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $15,098,838 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 130 firms, avg revenue/firm $987,029,962 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $177,764,096 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 345

caveats: This measures operator requirement for the current service basket, not whether the same independent operator retains the customer.
Some individualized production may migrate to pharmacies or healthcare facilities classified outside this NAICS.
o.change_evidence: Sponsor sourcing plans and technology assessments quantifying insourcing, point-of-care production, continuous manufacturing, product elimination, and software-only substitution would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Remaining demand for physical pharmaceutical preparations requires an accountable, compliant manufacturer to produce, test, package, document, and release each batch. AI can support workflows but cannot provide physical dosage forms or assume the firm's GMP accountability; a sponsor may insource or switch plants, but an operator remains necessary.

caveats: No representative contract dataset was available.
Retention differs between proprietary products, commodity generics, scarce capacity, and sponsor-owned formulations.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of supply and CDMO agreements showing cost-plus terms, price resets, rebates, productivity sharing, minimum volumes, requalification costs, and renewal outcomes would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Per-unit and per-batch pricing allows some savings retention, and qualification or technology-transfer costs impede immediate switching. Generic pricing pressure, sponsor bargaining, group purchasing, contract-manufacturing rebids, cost-plus clauses, service-level penalties, and customer continuous-improvement expectations should pass through a meaningful share over five years.

caveats: Implementation evidence for LMM pharmaceutical plants is not representative or publicly standardized.
Sterile, solid-dose, liquid, and specialty preparations have different validation and operational constraints.
rho.change_evidence: Validated deployment results showing labor hours, review accuracy, deviations, inspection findings, and cycle time for batch-record review, investigations, regulatory support, planning, and medical information would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: AI assistants should become useful in retrieval, drafting, planning, and exception prioritization, but GMP validation, data integrity, customer quality agreements, change control, cybersecurity, auditability, and required human checks constrain substitution. FDA notes that critical manufacturing steps require responsible performance and checking or verification of automated equipment.

caveats: No owner-age or succession panel specific to eligible firms was found.
Distressed closures and asset divestitures must be excluded unless a transferable going concern changes control.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, FDA establishment ownership changes, application-transfer records, and sponsor requalification events reconciled to the eligible firm denominator would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Qualified capacity, applications, customer approvals, technical capabilities, and supply relationships are transferable and attract strategic and financial buyers. However, observed five-year control-transfer data for eligible LMM firms were unavailable, and portfolio, application, and facility divestitures are often not company control transfers.

principal_driver: Recurring, physically indispensable drug production with a sizeable documentation and analytical labor layer.
principal_weakness: Regulated quality responsibility and validation sharply constrain how much exposed work can become removed labor.

sources.supports: For other chemical-manufacturing groups, lists major occupations such as equipment, packaging and mixing operators, supervisors, material movers, inspectors, chemists, and managers, used only as an occupation-mix proxy.
sources.title: BLS Data tables for OEWS industry charts

sources.supports: Details batch records, process validation, annual reviews, deviations, testing, yield calculations, equipment and facility controls, and responsible checks for critical manufacturing steps.
sources.title: FDA Dosage Form Drug Manufacturers CGMP Inspection Guide

sources.supports: States that FDA monitors manufacturer CGMP compliance and assesses whether firms have the facilities, equipment, and ability to manufacture intended drugs, including under 21 CFR Parts 210 and 211.
sources.title: FDA Current Good Manufacturing Practice Regulations

sources.supports: Identifies weak incentives for less-profitable drugs, lack of rewards for mature quality systems, and logistical and regulatory recovery challenges; reports that 42% of 2013-2017 shortage drugs had a significant production increase and 30% were fully restored.
sources.title: FDA Drug Shortages: Root Causes and Potential Solutions

sources.supports: Projects retail prescription-drug spending average growth of 4.9% during 2026-2033, used as a broader nominal downstream demand proxy.
sources.title: CMS National Health Expenditure Projections 2024-2033

title: Pharmaceutical Preparation Manufacturing
